title: Why Is Monstera Yellowing? Causes and Easy Fixes
excerpt: Discover the most common reasons why your monstera is yellowing and learn simple, actionable steps to restore your plant's lush, green leaves back to health.
content:
Why Is Your Monstera Yellowing? Here's What's Going On

Few things are more disheartening than watching your beautiful monstera develop yellow leaves. The good news? Yellowing is one of the most common monstera problems, and in most cases, it's completely fixable. Your plant is simply trying to communicate with you — and once you learn to decode the signs, you'll be well on your way to a thriving, deeply green monstera.

In this guide, we'll walk through every major reason why your monstera might be yellowing, how to diagnose the problem, and exactly what to do about it.

The Most Common Reasons Monstera Leaves Turn Yellow

1. Overwatering (The #1 Culprit)

Overwatering is by far the most frequent cause of monstera yellowing. When the roots sit in soggy soil for too long, they begin to suffocate and rot, which prevents the plant from absorbing the nutrients it needs. The result? Those telltale yellow leaves.

How to tell if overwatering is the problem:

  • The soil feels wet or soggy well after watering
  • Leaves are yellowing from the bottom up
  • You notice a musty smell coming from the soil
  • The pot feels unusually heavy

The fix: Allow the top 2 inches of soil to dry out completely before watering again. Make sure your pot has adequate pots with drainage holes, and consider switching to a well-draining quality potting mix. If root rot has set in, check out our guide on treating root rot to save your plant before it's too late.

2. Underwatering

On the flip side, too little water can also cause yellowing — though the signs look slightly different. Underwatered monsteras tend to develop yellow leaves that also appear dry, crispy, or curling at the edges.

The fix: Water your monstera thoroughly until water drains freely from the bottom of the pot. Establish a consistent watering schedule based on your home's humidity and season. A general rule of thumb is to water every 1–2 weeks in summer and every 2–3 weeks in winter.

3. Too Much or Too Little Light

Monstera plants thrive in bright, indirect light. When they're placed in a spot that's too dark, they can't photosynthesize efficiently, causing older leaves to turn yellow and drop. Direct sunlight, on the other hand, can scorch leaves — which may appear as yellow or bleached patches.

The fix: Move your monstera to a spot near a window that receives several hours of bright, indirect light each day. An east or north-facing window is often ideal. If your space is naturally dim, consider a grow light to supplement natural light.

4. Nutrient Deficiencies

A lack of key nutrients — particularly nitrogen, magnesium, or iron — can cause monstera leaves to turn yellow. This is especially common in plants that haven't been repotted in a long time or that are grown in nutrient-depleted soil.

Signs of nutrient deficiency:

  • Nitrogen deficiency: Uniform yellowing starting with older, lower leaves
  • Magnesium deficiency: Yellow patches between the leaf veins while veins stay green
  • Iron deficiency: Yellowing on new growth with visible green veins

The fix: Feed your monstera with a balanced liquid balanced liquid fertilizer every 4–6 weeks during the growing season (spring and summer). Reduce or stop fertilizing in fall and winter when growth slows. You can also explore our complete monstera fertilizing guide for more detailed advice.

5. Root Bound Plants

When a monstera outgrows its pot, its roots become cramped and compacted. This restricts the plant's ability to uptake water and nutrients, which can lead to yellowing leaves and stunted growth.

How to check: Look for roots growing out of the drainage holes or circling the surface of the soil. If you gently remove the plant from its pot and see a dense mass of roots, it's time to repot.

The fix: Repot your monstera into a container that is 2–3 inches larger in diameter than the current one. Spring is the best time to repot, as the plant is entering its active growing phase.

6. Temperature Stress and Cold Drafts

Monsteras are tropical plants that prefer temperatures between 65–85°F (18–30°C). Exposure to cold drafts from windows, air conditioning vents, or temperatures below 55°F (13°C) can cause stress that manifests as yellowing or browning leaves.

The fix: Keep your monstera away from cold windows in winter and air conditioning vents in summer. Avoid placing it in rooms that experience significant temperature fluctuations.

7. Pests

Common houseplant pests like spider mites, mealybugs, and scale insects can damage monstera leaves, causing them to turn yellow. These tiny invaders feed on the plant's sap, weakening it over time.

What to look for:

  • Fine webbing under leaves (spider mites)
  • White cottony clusters in leaf joints (mealybugs)
  • Sticky residue on leaves or nearby surfaces
  • Tiny moving dots on the undersides of leaves

The fix: Inspect your plant regularly, especially the undersides of leaves. Treat infestations with neem oil, insecticidal soap, or rubbing alcohol on a cotton swab. For a deeper dive, visit our guide on identifying and treating common monstera pests.

8. Natural Aging

Sometimes, there's nothing to worry about at all. It's completely normal for older, lower monstera leaves to turn yellow and die off as part of the plant's natural lifecycle. If only one or two lower leaves are yellowing while the rest of the plant looks healthy, this is likely just natural aging.

The fix: Simply remove the yellow leaves with clean, sharp scissors or pruning shears to keep your plant looking tidy and to prevent any potential spread of disease.

How to Diagnose Your Monstera's Yellow Leaves

Not sure which issue is affecting your plant? Try this simple checklist:

  1. Check the soil moisture — is it soggy, bone dry, or just right?
  2. Assess the light — is your plant getting bright, indirect light?
  3. Inspect for pests — look closely at the undersides of leaves
  4. Check the roots — is it time for a larger pot?
  5. Consider the season — have you been fertilizing regularly?
  6. Evaluate placement — is it near a cold draft or heating vent?

Working through this list will help you pinpoint the cause quickly, so you can get your monstera back on track.

Preventing Yellow Leaves in the Future

Prevention is always easier than cure. Here are a few key habits to keep your monstera looking its absolute best:

  • Water consistently and only when the top layer of soil has dried out
  • Fertilize regularly during the growing season
  • Repot every 1–2 years or when roots begin to crowd the pot
  • Maintain stable temperatures and avoid cold drafts
  • Dust the leaves occasionally so they can absorb light efficiently
  • Rotate the pot every few weeks for even light exposure

With the right care routine, your monstera will reward you with dramatic, glossy, deeply green foliage. If you want to go further, explore our full monstera care guide for everything you need to grow a truly spectacular plant.

Final Thoughts

Yellowing monstera leaves might feel alarming, but they're really just your plant's way of asking for help. In most cases, a simple adjustment to your watering habits, light placement, or feeding routine is all it takes to turn things around. Stay observant, act quickly when you notice changes, and don't be too hard on yourself — even experienced plant parents deal with yellow leaves from time to time.

Have more questions about your monstera? Browse the LeafLogic troubleshooting library for more plant problem guides, or drop your question in the comments below!
